text: Divide and Conquer Pattern: Core template. Split problem into subproblems, solve recursively, combine results. Template: def solve(input): if base_case(input): return trivial_solution; left, right = divide(input); left_result = solve(left); right_result = solve(right); return combine(left_result, right_result). Time: T(n) = aT(n/b) + O(n^d). Use Master Theorem to analyze.

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Master Theorem. T(n) = aT(n/b) + O(n^d). Compare n^(log_b(a)) vs n^d. Case 1: log_b(a) > d → T(n) = O(n^(log_b(a))). Case 2: log_b(a) == d → T(n) = O(n^d * log n). Case 3: log_b(a) < d → T(n) = O(n^d). Examples: Merge sort T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) → O(n log n). Binary search T(n)=T(n/2)+O(1) → O(log n).

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Merge sort. Divide array in half, recursively sort halves, merge sorted halves. Merge: two-pointer scan of sorted halves, always pick smaller element. Time: O(n log n), Space: O(n). Stable sort. Use for: sorting linked lists (no random access), external sorting, counting inversions. Foundation of many divide-and-conquer algorithms.

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Quick sort. Choose pivot, partition array so left < pivot < right. Recurse on both sides. Partition: two pointers or single pointer sweep. Average: O(n log n). Worst case: O(n²) for sorted input with bad pivot. Use random pivot or median-of-three to avoid worst case. In-place: O(log n) stack space average. Fastest general-purpose sort in practice.

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Maximum subarray (Kadane's vs D&C). D&C approach: max subarray either in left half, right half, or crosses midpoint. Crossing subarray: max suffix of left + max prefix of right. T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) → O(n log n). Kadane's DP is O(n) — better for maximum subarray alone. D&C is useful when problem requires divide step (e.g., during merge sort, also computing inversion count).

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Closest pair of points. Find two closest points in 2D. Naive: O(n²). D&C: sort by x. Divide at median x. Recursively find min distance in each half (δ = min(left_min, right_min)). Check strip of width 2δ around dividing line — only O(1) points per horizontal band. Combine: O(n). Total: O(n log n). Template: sort → divide → combine with strip check.

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Strassen's matrix multiplication. Standard: O(n³). Strassen: split n×n matrices into 2×2 blocks, use 7 multiplications (vs 8) of n/2 × n/2 submatrices. T(n)=7T(n/2)+O(n²) → O(n^2.807). In practice: overhead from additions makes it faster only for very large n. Modern algorithms (Coppersmith-Winograd): O(n^2.37). Important theoretically, rarely used directly.

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Count inversions. Count pairs (i,j) where i<j but arr[i]>arr[j]. Modified merge sort: during merge, when taking element from right half before finishing left half, add count of remaining left elements to total inversions. T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) → O(n log n). Use for: measuring sortedness, Kendall tau distance between rankings.

Divide and Conquer Pattern: QuickSelect (order statistics). Find kth smallest element. Partition around pivot. If pivot index == k-1: done. If k-1 < pivot_index: recurse left. Else: recurse right. Average: O(n). Worst case: O(n²) with bad pivot. Median-of-medians pivot selection gives O(n) worst case. Use for: find median, kth largest/smallest without full sort.

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Fast Fourier Transform (FFT). Evaluate polynomial at n roots of unity. Divide: split into even/odd indexed coefficients. T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) → O(n log n). Use for: polynomial multiplication in O(n log n) vs O(n²) naive, large number multiplication, convolution. Key insight: recursive evaluation at n/2 roots of unity using Cooley-Tukey butterfly operation.

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Median of medians. Select median of n elements in O(n) worst case. Divide into groups of 5. Find median of each group O(n). Recursively find median of medians. Use as pivot for partition. At least 3n/10 elements on each side. T(n)=T(n/5)+T(7n/10)+O(n) → O(n). Rarely used in practice (high constant) but important theoretically.

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Binary search as D&C. Divide sorted array in half. Compare middle element with target. Recurse on one half only. T(n)=T(n/2)+O(1) → O(log n). Classic example of D&C where only one subproblem is solved (no combine step needed). All binary search variants (leftmost, rightmost, rotated array) follow same D&C structure.

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Skyline problem. Find silhouette of buildings. D&C: divide buildings into halves, recursively get skylines, merge two skylines. Merge: two-pointer sweep using running max heights from each skyline. Add critical points where merged height changes. T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) → O(n log n). Alternative: segment tree or coordinate compression + sweep line.

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Tiling problems. Tile deficient 2^n × 2^n board with L-shaped trominoes (one cell missing). D&C: place one L-tromino at center covering one cell from each quadrant (except quadrant with missing cell). Recursively tile each quadrant. T(n)=4T(n/2)+O(1) → O(n). Elegant example of D&C with creative combination step.

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Power set via D&C. Generate all 2^n subsets. For n elements: take all subsets of first n-1 elements, add nth element to each. T(n) = 2T(n-1) + O(2^(n-1)) → O(2^n). Alternatively: bit masking generates all subsets iteratively. D&C approach naturally generates subsets and demonstrates exponential growth of recursion tree.

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Integer multiplication (Karatsuba). Multiply n-digit numbers. Split each into two halves: A=A1*10^(n/2)+A0, B=B1*10^(n/2)+B0. Three multiplications: z0=A0*B0, z2=A1*B1, z1=(A0+A1)*(B0+B1)-z0-z2. Result = z2*10^n + z1*10^(n/2) + z0. T(n)=3T(n/2)+O(n) → O(n^1.585) vs O(n²) naive. Faster for large integers.

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Convex hull (divide and conquer). Find smallest convex polygon containing all points. Sort by x. Divide into left/right halves. Recursively compute hull of each. Merge: find upper and lower tangent lines connecting two hulls. Walk along tangent lines to form merged hull. Time: O(n log n). Alternative: Graham scan, Jarvis march (gift wrapping).

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Sparse table (range minimum query). Precompute answers for all ranges of length 2^k. sparse[k][i] = min of arr[i..i+2^k-1]. Build: sparse[k][i] = min(sparse[k-1][i], sparse[k-1][i+2^(k-1)]). Query [l,r]: k=floor(log2(r-l+1)); min(sparse[k][l], sparse[k][r-2^k+1]). Time: O(n log n) build, O(1) query. Perfect overlap allowed for idempotent operations (min, max, gcd).

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Segment tree with lazy propagation. Divide array into segments. Each node stores aggregate (sum/min/max) for its range. Update: propagate lazy tag down when needed. Range query: combine results from relevant segments. Time: O(log n) update and query. Build: O(n). Use for: range sum with range updates, range minimum, any monoid operation with lazy updates.

Divide and Conquer Pattern: Parallel D&C (MapReduce). Divide data across machines, process locally, reduce/merge results. Example: word count — split documents, count locally, merge counts. D&C is naturally parallelizable when subproblems are independent. Used in: distributed sort (split, sort locally, merge), distributed matrix multiplication, parallel reduction operations.
